prompt: POSITIONING FRAME TEST, 2026. A founder said the relatable opener is: 'You shipped your product, and every enterprise deal needs a security audit.' Then the product (kolm) is 'on demand, reproducible, verifiable, end-to-end security audits without the lead time.' Evaluate: is anchoring on 'your product' (the thing the founder built and identifies with) + 'every enterprise deal needs a security audit' the right relatable situation? Is 'your product' better than 'your AI' / 'your AI agent' as the anchor for a founder, given kolm specifically audits the AI/agent part (from logs) and is AI-specific? How do we stay accurate (kolm audits the AI/agent, not a full-product pentest) while anchoring relatably on the product + the deal + the audit? Cite real 2026 founder language about 'every enterprise deal needs X' and security audits blocking deals. Recommend the exact relatable framing: what to anchor on, and how to bridge from 'your product / your deal' to 'we audit your AI on demand'.
content:
**Yes, anchoring on "your product" + "every enterprise deal needs a security audit" is the right relatable situation.** It directly mirrors 2026 founder and market reality: deals routinely stall or die in security/procurement reviews long after a verbal yes or successful demo, not on product fit or pricing.[[1]](https://www.ciphrix.com/blog/why-enterprise-deals-stall-at-security-review-and-how-to-prevent-it)[[2]](https://www.cyberbase.ai/blog/four-team-problem-enterprise-deals-die-in-security-reviews)[[3]](https://www.iteratorshq.com/blog/enterprise-readiness-for-startups-common-deal-blockers-at-security-review-legal-and-procurement/)

This pattern appears repeatedly in 2026 discussions:
- "Enterprise deals don't die on product or pricing—they die at security review."
- Deals die in the gap between "verbal yes" and signed contract due to security reviews taking weeks.
- "Same pattern in every enterprise deal: AI team ships a POC, security blocks production."
- AI startups lose deals in security review (not product); every enterprise deal triggers security questions/AI governance items in questionnaires.[[4]](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/ezgituran_ai-startup-enterprise-activity-7458262865829900288-S0Lh)[[5]](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/alexsalazar_most-enterprises-want-to-deploy-ai-agents-activity-7431831552222875649-j8Nj)[[6]](https://www.orbiqhq.com/use-cases/saas)

Security reviews (SOC 2, questionnaires, pen tests, AI-specific governance) are table stakes and frequent blockers for enterprise sales, especially AI/agent products. Anchoring here creates immediate recognition for founders selling into enterprises.

**'Your product' is generally better than 'your AI'/'your AI agent' as the primary anchor.** Founders strongly identify with the thing they shipped and are selling—the full product or feature set—rather than narrowing immediately to the AI/agent component. "You shipped your product" feels personal, concrete, and broad enough to encompass the deal context. "Your AI" risks feeling narrower or more technical early on, potentially reducing relatability unless the entire product *is* positioned as an AI agent/tool.

kolm is AI-specific (audits the agent/AI part from logs), so the framing must bridge accurately without overstating it as a full-product pentest or general security audit. The relatable opener works because the security audit requirement applies to the deal involving the product; kolm then slots in as the fast, targeted solution for the AI/agent element that often surfaces in those reviews (e.g., AI governance, output controls, auditability, data flows).

**Recommended exact relatable framing** (testable opener + bridge):

**Opener/anchor:** "You shipped your product, and every enterprise deal needs a security audit."

**Bridge to kolm (accurate + specific):** "...especially for the AI/agent powering it. Kolm gives you on-demand, reproducible, verifiable end-to-end security audits of your AI/agent—from logs, without the weeks of lead time."

This keeps the emotional/relatable hook on the shipped product + universal deal friction (widely echoed in 2026 founder language), while precisely scoping kolm to the AI/agent audit. It avoids claiming a full pentest or general security solution. Variants like "You shipped your [product/AI feature]" or "Your AI agent is live in the product, and every enterprise deal..." can be A/B tested depending on the audience, but lead with the product for broadest founder identification.

This positioning stays truthful, leverages real market pain, and positions kolm as the fast, targeted unlock rather than a generic audit tool.
